packs.name: bluetemberg-guardrails-git
packs.description: Git guardrails for Bluetemberg — enforce conventional branch names on AI-created worktrees.

guardrails.name: conventional-branch-names
guardrails.description: Enforce conventional branch names before creating a worktree

packs.preview: Blocks AI tools from creating worktrees with auto-generated names (e.g. `claude/some-name`). Requires a branch name in `type/description` format before the worktree is created.

packs.name: bluetemberg-rules-agent-memory
packs.description: Agent memory rules for Bluetemberg — statelessness as a hard constraint, memory provenance, promotion through consolidation, and authority-ranked recall.

rules.name: memory-architecture-checklist
rules.description: Treat LLM statelessness as a hard constraint — decide explicitly what state survives a turn, which storage tier holds it, and how it is evicted.

rules.name: memory-promotion
rules.description: Session observations never write directly to durable memory — promotion runs through a reviewable consolidation step that dedupes, supersedes, and ratifies.

rules.name: memory-provenance
rules.description: Every durable memory carries provenance — source, time, confidence, and whether it was user-stated or agent-inferred. No silent writes.

rules.name: memory-recall-authority
rules.description: Rank recalled memory by authority, not only relevance — and never let stale, contradicted, or superseded memory present as a current constraint.

packs.preview: An LLM call is stateless. Nothing carries between turns except what you put back into the context window. Continuity is an engineering decision, not a model feature — and provider-managed memory APIs delegate the storage, not the decision. - For every piece of state, decide explicitly whether it mus

packs.name: bluetemberg-rules-context-engineering
packs.description: Context engineering rules for Bluetemberg — structuring LLM context, managing token budgets, avoiding context pollution.

rules.name: context-pollution-prevention
rules.description: Prevent context pollution — scope tool calls to what you need, don't inject noise into the conversation.

rules.name: context-positioning
rules.description: Place load-bearing content at the edges of the context, not the middle — models access the start and end far better than the middle.

rules.name: context-window-budget
rules.description: Manage context window budget explicitly — prune stale content, compress retrieved data, never let context grow unbounded.

rules.name: multi-turn-context-hygiene
rules.description: Don't keep talking to a derailed conversation — consolidate the requirements and restart fresh. Multi-turn degradation is large and doesn't self-correct.

rules.name: multi-turn-state-management
rules.description: Manage state across multi-turn interactions — track what was decided, what changed, and what is still open.

rules.name: prompt-structure
rules.description: Structure prompts into labeled, delimited sections — separate instructions from data, and keep persistent rules in the system message.

packs.preview: Context pollution is when irrelevant, contradictory, or duplicate information crowds out the signal needed for accurate reasoning. This is not a soft concern: adding even a **single distractor** — plausibly-related but irrelevant content — measurably lowers accuracy, and across frontier models a con

packs.name: bluetemberg-rules-devops
packs.description: Infrastructure rules for Bluetemberg — Docker, Terraform, Ansible, Kubernetes, Helm, CI/CD, shell scripts, and idempotency.

rules.name: ansible-conventions
rules.description: Follow Ansible best practices for idempotent, maintainable infrastructure automation.

rules.name: ci-workflow-conventions
rules.description: Secure CI/CD workflows with minimal permissions, pinned actions, and OIDC authentication.

rules.name: container-image-pinning
rules.description: Pin container image versions to prevent unexpected breaking changes.

rules.name: docker-best-practices
rules.description: Follow container best practices for secure, efficient Docker images.

rules.name: helm-conventions
rules.description: Write safe, reusable Helm charts with secure defaults and consistent values structure.

rules.name: idempotency
rules.description: Every operation — task, script, or deployment — must be safe to re-run without side effects.

rules.name: jinja2-templates
rules.description: Write safe, readable Jinja2 templates for Ansible and configuration generation.

rules.name: kubernetes-manifests
rules.description: Write safe, production-grade Kubernetes manifests with resource limits, health checks, and security context.

rules.name: runbook-discipline
rules.description: Keep operational runbooks and ADRs accurate and commit them alongside the changes they document.

rules.name: shell-script-standards
rules.description: Write safe, portable shell scripts with error handling and clear control flow.

rules.name: terraform-conventions
rules.description: Follow Terraform module structure and naming conventions.

packs.preview: Write Ansible roles and playbooks that are idempotent, readable, and maintainable. - Use fully qualified collection names (FQCN): `ansible.builtin.copy` not `copy`. - Every task must have a descriptive `name:`. - Avoid `shell` and `command` modules; prefer native modules. - When `shell`/`command` is

packs.name: bluetemberg-rules-docs
packs.description: Documentation and diagnostics rules for Bluetemberg — docs parity, post-edit diagnostics, Mermaid diagrams.

rules.name: docs-parity
rules.description: Keep documentation in sync with every user-facing change.

rules.name: mermaid-diagrams
rules.description: Use Mermaid diagrams instead of ASCII art.

rules.name: post-edit-diagnostics
rules.description: Run diagnostics and formatter after editing code files.

packs.preview: A PR that changes behavior without updating docs is not done. - New or removed CLI flags, commands, or options - Changes to user-facing workflows (new prompts, removed choices, changed defaults) - New or removed public APIs, config options, or schema fields - Changes to output behavior or generated

packs.name: bluetemberg-rules-git
packs.description: Git workflow rules for Bluetemberg — branch protection, naming, PR workflow, git move, pre-commit checks.

rules.name: git-move
rules.description: Use git mv for tracked files to preserve history.

rules.name: git-workflow
rules.description: Branch protection, branch naming, and PR workflow rules.

rules.name: pre-commit-checks
rules.description: Run formatter, linter, and build checks before every commit.

packs.preview: If moving an existing file that is not `git ignored`, use `git mv {source} {dest}`, as this preserves the git history for the file and shows any new diff.

packs.name: bluetemberg-rules-llm-api-product
packs.description: LLM API and product-engineering rules for Bluetemberg — streaming, cost accounting, and cost-aware model selection.

rules.name: cost-accounting
rules.description: Measure LLM cost from the authoritative usage object and the provider's cost API — never from your own token estimates.

rules.name: model-selection-and-routing
rules.description: Pick models on the accuracy-vs-cost frontier and route easy queries to cheaper models — the most expensive model is rarely the right default.

rules.name: streaming
rules.description: Stream for perceived latency, but buffer tool-call JSON until the block closes and don't gate safety on partial output.

packs.preview: If an LLM feature has unit economics, measure them from the **authoritative numbers the provider returns**, not from estimates you compute yourself. Self-counted token estimates routinely miss what actually drives the bill: prompt-cache reads and writes, tool-use tokens, system-prompt overhead, and

packs.name: bluetemberg-rules-nextjs
packs.description: Next.js rules for Bluetemberg — NEXT_PUBLIC_* env var safety.

rules.name: nextjs-data-fetching
rules.description: Prefer fetch with cache options in Server Components over useEffect + API calls

rules.name: nextjs-image-optimization
rules.description: Always use next/image; never raw img tags

rules.name: nextjs-metadata
rules.description: Define metadata/generateMetadata per page; never use <Head> in App Router

rules.name: nextjs-public-env-vars
rules.description: NEXT_PUBLIC_* are inlined into the bundle at build time — never use for secrets or per-environment values.

rules.name: nextjs-server-components
rules.description: Default to React Server Components; use 'use client' only at leaf interactive nodes

packs.preview: In App Router, data fetching belongs in Server Components using async/await with the extended `fetch` API. `useEffect` + client-side API calls add a waterfall (page load → render → fetch → render again) and send a round-trip that a Server Component can eliminate entirely. - **Fetch data in Server Co

packs.name: bluetemberg-rules-security
packs.description: Security guardrail rules for Bluetemberg — secrets management, environment file protection, API error handling.

rules.name: api-error-handling
rules.description: Use structured error responses and never leak internal details.

rules.name: llm-package-hallucination
rules.description: Verify every LLM-suggested dependency exists in the registry before installing — hallucinated package names are a real supply-chain attack surface (slopsquatting).

rules.name: never-read-env
rules.description: Never read .env files directly in code.

rules.name: security-secrets
rules.description: Never hardcode secrets, tokens, or credentials in source code.

packs.preview: All API endpoints must return structured, consistent error responses. Never expose stack traces, internal paths, or database details to clients. Always return errors with a consistent shape: ```json { "error": { "code": "NOT_FOUND", "message": "Resource not found" } } ``` - Map internal errors to ap

packs.name: bluetemberg-rules-testing
packs.description: Testing rules for Bluetemberg — determinism, isolation, and meaningful coverage.

rules.name: coverage-meaningful-not-vanity
rules.description: Assert observable behavior and edge cases; never write tests that execute code without asserting anything

rules.name: flaky-test-policy
rules.description: Quarantine-then-fix flaky tests; a retried test is a tracked bug, not a pass

rules.name: mocking-boundaries
rules.description: Mock at I/O and network boundaries only; never mock the unit under test; prefer fakes over deep mock chains

rules.name: test-data-builders
rules.description: Use factory or builder functions for test fixtures; never scatter inline object literals across the test suite

rules.name: test-determinism
rules.description: Avoid timing-based, random, and wall-clock assertions; use fake timers and frozen clocks instead

rules.name: test-isolation
rules.description: Each test must set up and tear down its own state; never rely on execution order or shared mutable state

rules.name: test-naming
rules.description: Name tests as "it does X when Y" — describe the behavior under a condition, not the implementation

packs.preview: A line-coverage number tells you which lines were touched, not whether the software works. Tests that execute code without asserting outcomes inflate coverage metrics while providing no safety net — they are worse than no tests because they create false confidence. Coverage is a byproduct of writing

packs.name: bluetemberg-rules-typescript
packs.description: TypeScript code quality rules for Bluetemberg — type safety, coding standards, early returns, no console.log, design system reuse.

rules.name: coding-standards
rules.description: Keep functions and components small, readable, and easy to reason about.

rules.name: design-system-reuse
rules.description: Reuse existing shared UI components and design tokens before creating new ones.

rules.name: early-returns
rules.description: Prefer early returns and guard clauses over nested conditionals.

rules.name: no-console-log
rules.description: Forbid console.log in production code; use a logger instead.

rules.name: type-safety
rules.description: Enforce strict type safety — no implicit any, no unguarded assertions.

packs.preview: Keep functions and components small, readable, and easy to reason about. - Prefer small, single-purpose functions. - Keep control flow shallow; avoid deep nesting. - Use early returns and guard clauses to reduce `else` blocks. - Extract complex logic into well-named helpers. - Favor descriptive name
